groups.label: Actions
groups.blurb: Buttons, FABs, and other ways to trigger an action

groups.label: Containment
groups.blurb: Cards, lists, sheets, and dividers that hold content

groups.label: Navigation
groups.blurb: Bars, rails, drawers, tabs, and menus

groups.label: Selection & Inputs
groups.blurb: Checkboxes, switches, sliders, chips, and pickers

groups.label: Layout
groups.blurb: Row, Column, Box, and arrangement primitives

groups.label: Gestures & Scroll
groups.blurb: Click, drag, swipe, and scroll handling

components.name: Button
components.summary: Five emphasis levels from filled to text
components.description: Buttons let people take action. Material 3 ships five emphasis levels — filled (highest), tonal, elevated, outlined, and text (lowest) — plus support for leading icons and disabled states.

examples.title: Emphasis levels
examples.description: Pick emphasis to match a button's importance on the screen.

examples.title: Leading icon
Disabled
examples.code:
Button(onClick = {}, enabled = false) { Text("Filled") }
OutlinedButton(onClick = {}, enabled = false) { Text("Outlined") }

components.name: Icon Button
components.summary: Standard, filled, outlined, and toggle icon buttons
components.description: Icon buttons trigger a single action with a compact, recognizable glyph. They come in standard, filled, and outlined styles, plus a toggleable variant.

examples.title: Styles
Toggle
examples.description: An IconToggleButton flips between checked and unchecked.

components.name: Floating Action Button
components.summary: Small, regular, large, and extended FABs
components.description: The FAB represents the primary action of a screen. It comes in small, regular, and large sizes, plus an extended form that pairs an icon with a label.

examples.title: Sizes & extended

components.name: Badge
components.summary: Dot, count, and overflow badges anchored to an icon
components.description: A Badge draws attention to a small piece of dynamic status — an unread count or a simple dot — on top of another element. Wrap an anchor in a BadgedBox and supply the badge; an empty Badge renders as a dot, while content renders as a label.

examples.title: Dot, count & overflow
examples.description: An empty Badge is a dot; pass Text for a count, capping large values at 99+.

examples.title: Live counter
examples.description: Drive the badge from state and hide it when the count is zero.

components.name: Progress Indicators
components.summary: Indeterminate and determinate circular & linear progress
components.description: Progress indicators express an unspecified wait time (indeterminate) or how far along a known operation is (determinate). Both circular and linear variants are available; pass a progress lambda for the determinate form.

examples.title: Indeterminate
examples.description: Omit progress for a continuous, looping animation.
examples.code:
CircularProgressIndicator()
LinearProgressIndicator()

examples.title: Determinate
examples.description: Animate the progress value and surface it as a percentage.

components.name: Snackbar
components.summary: Brief, transient messages with an optional action
components.description: A Snackbar shows a short, low-priority message at the bottom of the screen. Host it with a SnackbarHost bound to a SnackbarHostState, then call showSnackbar from a coroutine. An action label lets people respond, and the returned result tells you whether they tapped it.

examples.title: Show a message
examples.description: Launch showSnackbar from a coroutine scope tied to the composition.

examples.title: Action & result
examples.description: Read the SnackbarResult to know whether the action was tapped.

components.name: Dialog
components.summary: AlertDialog confirmations and fully custom dialogs
components.description: Dialogs interrupt to request a decision or show critical information. AlertDialog gives you a ready-made layout with icon, title, body, and confirm/dismiss buttons, while the lower-level Dialog lets you place any content — such as a Card — in a modal window.

examples.title: AlertDialog
examples.description: A structured confirmation with confirm and dismiss buttons.

examples.title: Custom dialog
examples.description: Wrap any content — here a Card — in the low-level Dialog.

components.name: Tooltip
components.summary: Plain tooltips anchored to an interactive element
components.description: Tooltips reveal a brief label for an element on long-press or hover. A TooltipBox positions a PlainTooltip relative to its anchor and is driven by a TooltipState that controls when the tooltip appears.

examples.title: Plain tooltip
examples.description: Anchor a PlainTooltip to an IconButton via TooltipBox.

components.name: Card
components.summary: Filled, elevated, and outlined containers for grouped content
components.description: Cards hold a single coherent piece of content and actions. Material 3 offers three styles — filled, elevated, and outlined — and cards can be made clickable to act as tappable list items.

examples.title: Styles
examples.description: Filled, elevated, and outlined share the same content slot.

examples.title: Clickable card
examples.description: Pass onClick to make the whole card a tappable row.

components.name: List Item
components.summary: Headline, supporting text, and leading content rows
components.description: ListItem renders a single Material 3 list row with slots for headline, supporting text, and leading or trailing content. Wrap a column of them in a tonal Surface and separate them with dividers to build a settings-style list.

examples.title: List with dividers

components.name: Modal Bottom Sheet
components.summary: A sheet that slides up from the bottom edge
components.description: A modal bottom sheet presents a focused set of actions anchored to the bottom of the screen, dimming the content behind it. Drive it with rememberModalBottomSheetState and hide it before clearing the open flag for a smooth exit animation.

examples.title: Open and dismiss
examples.description: A Button opens the sheet; selecting an action animates it closed.

components.name: Divider
components.summary: Horizontal and vertical thin separating lines
components.description: Dividers are thin lines that group content into sections. Use a HorizontalDivider between stacked items and a VerticalDivider inside a fixed-height row to separate side-by-side content.

examples.title: Horizontal
Vertical
examples.description: A VerticalDivider needs a bounded height from its parent row.

components.name: Top App Bar
components.summary: Small and center-aligned bars with navigation and actions
components.description: Top app bars display a screen title plus a leading navigation icon and trailing action icons. Material 3 ships small, center-aligned, medium, and large variants; the small and center-aligned forms are shown here.

examples.title: Small top app bar
examples.description: A title aligned to the start, with navigation and action icons.

examples.title: Center-aligned top app bar
examples.description: The title is centered, ideal for top-level screens.

components.name: Navigation Bar
components.summary: Bottom bar with three to five destinations
components.description: A navigation bar lets people switch between top-level destinations from the bottom of the screen. Each item carries an icon and a label, and exactly one is selected at a time.

examples.title: Four destinations
examples.description: Tap an item to move the selection.

components.name: Navigation Rail
components.summary: Vertical destination switcher for wide screens
components.description: A navigation rail places top-level destinations along the side of the screen, a common pattern on tablets and other large displays. Like the navigation bar, exactly one destination is selected at a time.

examples.title: Three destinations
examples.description: The rail is anchored to the start edge inside a fixed-height box.

examples.title: Adaptive: bar ↔ rail
examples.description: A NavigationBar in compact width swaps to a NavigationRail when

components.name: Navigation Drawer
components.summary: A drawer sheet of destination items
components.description: A navigation drawer presents destinations in a vertical sheet that usually slides in from the side. Shown here is the drawer sheet content on its own — a column of NavigationDrawerItem entries — without the full sliding overlay.

examples.title: Drawer sheet content
examples.description: Each item pairs a label and icon, with one selected.

components.name: Tabs
components.summary: Fixed and scrollable tab rows
components.description: Tabs organize content across parallel sections of a screen. A fixed TabRow splits the available width evenly, while a ScrollableTabRow keeps each tab at its natural width and scrolls horizontally when they overflow.

examples.title: Fixed tabs
examples.description: Three evenly spaced tabs with content below.

examples.title: Scrollable tabs
examples.description: Many tabs scroll horizontally rather than compressing.

components.name: Menu
components.summary: Dropdown menus and exposed dropdown selection
components.description: Menus display a list of choices on a temporary surface. A DropdownMenu anchors to a trigger such as a button, while an ExposedDropdownMenuBox pairs a text field with a menu so people can pick a value.

examples.title: Dropdown menu
examples.description: A menu anchored to a button via an expanded flag.

examples.title: Exposed dropdown
examples.description: A read-only text field reveals a menu of selectable values.

components.name: Bottom App Bar
components.summary: Bottom bar pairing actions with a FAB
components.description: A bottom app bar groups a small set of action icons at the bottom of the screen and commonly hosts a floating action button for the screen's primary action.

examples.title: Actions with a FAB
examples.description: Leading action icons plus a trailing floating action button.

components.name: Checkbox
components.summary: Single, grouped, and tri-state parent checkboxes
components.description: Checkboxes let people select one or more items from a set, or turn a single option on or off. A TriStateCheckbox can act as a parent that reflects and toggles a group of child checkboxes.

examples.title: Parent / child group
examples.description: A TriStateCheckbox shows On, Off, or Indeterminate for its children.

components.name: Switch
components.summary: Toggle a single setting on or off
components.description: Switches toggle the state of a single item on or off. They are the preferred control for settings rows where the change takes effect immediately.

examples.title: Settings rows
examples.description: Pair each switch with a label in a Row.

components.name: Radio Button
components.summary: Pick exactly one option from a set
components.description: Radio buttons let people select a single option from a mutually exclusive set. Wrap each row in Modifier.selectable so the whole row is tappable and accessible.

examples.title: Selectable group
examples.description: Make the entire row toggle the selection, not just the dot.

components.name: Slider
components.summary: Continuous, stepped, and range sliders
components.description: Sliders let people select a value or range from along a track. They support continuous motion, discrete steps within a range, and a two-thumb range variant.

examples.title: Continuous
examples.description: A free-moving thumb across the default 0..1 range.

examples.title: Stepped
examples.description: steps places discrete tick marks inside the value range.
examples.code:
var value by remember { mutableStateOf(2f) }
Slider(
    value = value,
    onValueChange = { value = it },
    valueRange = 0f..10f,
    steps = 9
)
Text("Step: ${value.roundToInt()}")

examples.title: Range
examples.description: A RangeSlider exposes two thumbs for a lower and upper bound.

components.name: Chip
components.summary: Assist, filter, input, and suggestion chips
components.description: Chips help people enter information, make selections, filter content, or trigger actions. Material 3 ships four kinds: assist, filter, input, and suggestion.

examples.title: Assist
examples.description: An AssistChip triggers an action related to nearby content.

examples.title: Filter (multi-select)
examples.description: FilterChips reflect selection state and can be toggled independently.

examples.title: Input (removable)
examples.description: An InputChip represents a discrete entry the user can dismiss.

examples.title: Suggestion
examples.description: SuggestionChips surface dynamically generated recommendations.

components.name: Segmented Button
components.summary: Single- and multi-choice segmented selection
components.description: Segmented buttons group related options in a compact toggle. Use single choice for mutually exclusive options and multi choice for independent toggles.

examples.title: Single choice
examples.description: Exactly one segment is selected at a time.

examples.title: Multi choice
examples.description: Any combination of segments can be active at once.

components.name: Date & Time Pickers
components.summary: Inline date picker, date dialog, and time picker
components.description: Pickers let people choose a date or time. Material 3 provides an inline DatePicker, a DatePickerDialog for modal selection, and a TimePicker with a clock face.

examples.title: Inline date picker
examples.description: Embed a DatePicker directly in the layout.
examples.code:
val state = rememberDatePickerState()
DatePicker(state = state)

examples.title: Date picker dialog
examples.description: A Button opens a modal dialog and shows the picked date.

examples.title: Time picker
examples.description: A TimePicker with a clock face reflects the chosen time.
examples.code:
val state = rememberTimePickerState(initialHour = 9, initialMinute = 30)
TimePicker(state = state)
Text(String.format(Locale.US, "%02d:%02d", state.hour, state.minute))

components.name: Text & Typography
components.summary: Type scale, annotated spans, overflow, and selection
components.description: Text renders strings using the Material 3 type scale. It supports rich, inline styling via AnnotatedString, truncation with TextOverflow, and user text selection through SelectionContainer.

examples.title: Type scale
examples.description: The Material 3 type scale ranges from display down to label.
examples.code:
Text("Display", style = MaterialTheme.typography.displaySmall)
Text("Headline", style = MaterialTheme.typography.headlineSmall)
Text("Title", style = MaterialTheme.typography.titleMedium)
Text("Body", style = MaterialTheme.typography.bodyMedium)
Text("Label", style = MaterialTheme.typography.labelMedium)

examples.title: Annotated spans
examples.description: buildAnnotatedString applies bold, italic, underline, and color spans.

examples.title: Overflow
examples.description: Limit lines and truncate the remainder with an ellipsis.
examples.code:
Text(
    text = longText,
    maxLines = 2,
    overflow = TextOverflow.Ellipsis
)

examples.title: Selection
examples.description: Wrap text in a SelectionContainer to make it user-selectable.

components.name: Text Field
components.summary: Filled, outlined, error, password, search, and multi-line entry
components.description: Text fields let people enter and edit text. Material 3 offers filled and outlined styles with labels, placeholders, supporting text, error states, leading and trailing icons, keyboard options, and visual transformations.

examples.title: Filled
examples.description: A filled field with label, placeholder, and a Clear action when filled.

examples.title: Error & validation
examples.description: An outlined field that flags an invalid email via isError and supportingText.

examples.title: Password
examples.description: Toggle a visual transformation to show or hide the entry.

examples.title: Search
examples.description: A leading Search icon hints at the field's purpose.
examples.code:
var query by rememberSaveable { mutableStateOf("") }
OutlinedTextField(
    value = query,
    onValueChange = { query = it },
    label = { Text("Search") },
    leadingIcon = { Icon(Icons.Filled.Search, null) },
    singleLine = true
)

examples.title: Multi-line
examples.description: Set minLines to reserve room for several lines of input.
examples.code:
var notes by rememberSaveable { mutableStateOf("") }
OutlinedTextField(
    value = notes,
    onValueChange = { notes = it },
    label = { Text("Notes") },
    minLines = 3
)

components.name: Row & Column
components.summary: Lay children out horizontally or vertically
components.description: Row and Column are the workhorse layouts. Children are placed along the main axis; weight distributes leftover space proportionally, and arrangement controls the gaps between and around them.

examples.title: Weighted Row
examples.description: Weights split the available width — here 1:2:1.

examples.title: Column arrangement
examples.description: SpaceBetween pushes the first and last child to the edges.

components.name: Box
components.summary: Stack children and align them with Modifier.align
components.description: Box draws its children on top of one another, sized to the largest. Each child positions itself within the Box using Modifier.align, making it ideal for overlays, badges, and corner-anchored content.

examples.title: Aligned children
examples.description: Three labels anchored to different corners of the same Box.

components.name: Spacer
components.summary: Push siblings apart with flexible empty space
components.description: Spacer is an empty layout used to insert gaps. Given a weight inside a Row or Column it absorbs all leftover space, shoving the elements on either side to opposite ends.

examples.title: Push apart
examples.description: A weighted Spacer separates the leading and trailing text.

components.name: Surface
components.summary: Elevation, shape, and color for a sheet of content
components.description: Surface is the foundational container behind most Material components. It applies a background color, clips to a shape, and raises content with tonal elevation that tints the surface toward the primary color.

examples.title: Elevation, shape & color
examples.description: Different tonal elevations, shapes, and container colors.

components.name: Lazy Column
components.summary: Vertically scrolling list that composes items on demand
components.description: LazyColumn lays out and composes only the items that are visible, making it efficient for long or unbounded lists. Pair items with ListItem and HorizontalDivider for a classic settings-style list.

examples.title: List of items
examples.description: A fixed-height LazyColumn of ListItems separated by dividers.

components.name: Lazy Row
components.summary: Horizontally scrolling list of fixed-size cards
components.description: LazyRow is the horizontal counterpart to LazyColumn. It is well suited to carousels of cards or thumbnails, with Arrangement.spacedBy adding consistent gaps between items.

examples.title: Card carousel
examples.description: A LazyRow of fixed-size cards with spacing between them.

components.name: Lazy Grid
components.summary: Two-dimensional grid with a fixed column count
components.description: LazyVerticalGrid arranges items into columns described by GridCells. A GridCells.Fixed value pins the column count, while aspectRatio keeps each cell square.

examples.title: Fixed three-column grid
examples.description: A fixed-height grid of square, colored cells.

components.name: Staggered Grid
components.summary: Masonry-style grid where items have varying heights
components.description: LazyVerticalStaggeredGrid packs items of different heights into columns, creating a masonry layout. StaggeredGridCells.Fixed sets the column count.

examples.title: Masonry layout
examples.description: A fixed-height staggered grid of items with varying heights.

components.name: Pager
components.summary: Swipeable pages with a state-driven page count
components.description: HorizontalPager lays out swipeable pages backed by a PagerState. The state exposes currentPage, which can drive indicator dots beneath the pager.

examples.title: Horizontal pager with dots
examples.description: Four swipeable pages with indicator dots reflecting the current page.

components.name: animate*AsState
components.summary: Animate a single value whenever its target changes
components.description: The animate*AsState family interpolates a value toward a new target whenever that target changes. Swap in animateColorAsState, animateDpAsState, and friends to drive size, color, offset, or alpha — optionally with a spring for a bouncy feel.

examples.title: Color & size
examples.description: Tap to toggle; color and size animate to their new targets.

components.name: AnimatedVisibility
components.summary: Animate content as it enters and leaves composition
components.description: AnimatedVisibility animates the appearance and disappearance of its content. Combine enter transitions like fadeIn and expandVertically with matching exits such as fadeOut and shrinkVertically to control how content slides into view.

examples.title: Fade & expand
examples.description: Toggle to fade and expand the panel in and out.

components.name: AnimatedContent
components.summary: Animate the swap between two content states
components.description: AnimatedContent animates the transition whenever its targetState changes, cross-fading (and optionally sliding) between the old and new content. It is well suited to swapping a number, label, or whole layout in response to state.

examples.title: Counter
examples.description: The number animates as it changes with the +/- buttons.

components.name: Crossfade
components.summary: Cross-fade between mutually exclusive screens
components.description: Crossfade fades between two layouts based on a state value. It is the simplest way to animate a swap between mutually exclusive content, such as paging through a set of cards or switching between loading and loaded states.

examples.title: Pages
examples.description: Advance through three pages; each cross-fades into the next.

components.name: InfiniteTransition
components.summary: Loop an animation indefinitely
components.description: rememberInfiniteTransition drives one or more values that animate forever. Pair it with infiniteRepeatable and RepeatMode.Reverse to ping-pong a value back and forth — perfect for pulsing, breathing, or shimmering effects.

examples.title: Pulse
examples.description: An infinitely repeating tween reverses to pulse the alpha.
examples.code:
val transition = rememberInfiniteTransition(label = "pulse")
val alpha by transition.animateFloat(
    initialValue = 1f,
    targetValue = 0.2f,
    animationSpec = infiniteRepeatable(
        animation = tween(800),
        repeatMode = RepeatMode.Reverse
    ),
    label = "alpha"
)
Box(
    Modifier.size(96.dp).alpha(alpha)
        .clip(RoundedCornerShape(16.dp))
        .background(Color(0xFF6750A4))
)

components.name: Clickable
components.summary: Modifier.clickable and combinedClickable for taps and long presses
components.description: Modifier.clickable makes any composable respond to taps. combinedClickable adds long-press and double-tap callbacks, letting a plain Box behave like a button.

examples.title: Tap and long press
examples.description: combinedClickable distinguishes a tap from a long press.

components.name: Draggable
components.summary: Drag a box along one axis with rememberDraggableState
components.description: Modifier.draggable reports drag deltas on a single orientation. Track the accumulated offset in state, clamp it to a range, and apply it with Modifier.offset.

examples.title: Horizontal drag
examples.description: The handle follows your finger, clamped to a 0..220 dp range.

components.name: Transformable
components.summary: Pan, zoom, and rotate with rememberTransformableState
components.description: Modifier.transformable feeds combined pan, zoom, and rotation gestures into a single callback. Store the result in state and render it through a graphicsLayer.

examples.title: Pan, zoom, rotate
examples.description: Use two fingers to scale and rotate; drag to pan.

components.name: Scroll
components.summary: Modifier.verticalScroll over a fixed-height Column
components.description: For a known, modest number of items a plain Column made scrollable with Modifier.verticalScroll(rememberScrollState()) is simpler than a lazy list.

examples.title: Scrollable Column
examples.description: All rows are composed eagerly; the viewport is capped at 180 dp.

components.name: Swipe to Dismiss
components.summary: Swipe list items away with SwipeToDismissBox
components.description: SwipeToDismissBox wraps each row with a swipe gesture and a background that shows behind it. Confirm the dismissal to remove the backing item from the list.

examples.title: Dismiss to remove
examples.description: Swipe a row in either direction to delete it.

components.name: Pull to Refresh
components.summary: Pull down to trigger a refresh with PullToRefreshBox
components.description: PullToRefreshBox shows a refresh indicator when the content is pulled past the threshold. Drive it with an isRefreshing flag that you flip off when the work completes.

examples.title: Pull to refresh
examples.description: Pull down; the list reloads after a brief simulated fetch.

components.name: Canvas
components.summary: Draw lines, circles, rects, arcs, and paths
components.description: Canvas gives you a DrawScope to render custom graphics imperatively. The scope exposes drawLine, drawCircle, drawRect, drawArc, and drawPath, all working in pixels with the composable's own size available as this.size.

examples.title: Primitive shapes
examples.description: A single DrawScope draws a line, circle, rectangle, arc, and a triangle path.
examples.code:
Canvas(Modifier.fillMaxWidth().height(160.dp)) {
    drawLine(
        color = Color(0xFF6750A4),
        start = Offset(0f, 0f),
        end = Offset(size.width, size.height),
        strokeWidth = 4f
    )
    drawCircle(Color(0xFF7D5260), radius = 36f, center = Offset(60f, 60f))
    drawRect(Color(0xFF625B71), topLeft = Offset(120f, 24f), size = Size(72f, 72f))
    drawArc(
        color = Color(0xFF386A20),
        startAngle = 0f,
        sweepAngle = 270f,
        useCenter = true,
        topLeft = Offset(size.width - 120f, 24f),
        size = Size(80f, 80f)
    )
    val triangle = Path().apply {
        moveTo(size.width / 2f, size.height - 16f)
        lineTo(size.width / 2f - 48f, size.height - 80f)
        lineTo(size.width / 2f + 48f, size.height - 80f)
        close()
    }
    drawPath(triangle, Color(0xFFB3261E))
}

components.name: Gradients
components.summary: Linear, radial, and sweep brushes
components.description: A Brush describes how to paint an area. Brush.linearGradient interpolates along a vector, Brush.radialGradient fans out from a center, and Brush.sweepGradient sweeps around it. Any brush can fill a composable via Modifier.background.

examples.title: Three brush types
examples.description: Each box clips to a rounded shape and fills with a different gradient.
examples.code:
val colors = listOf(Color(0xFF6750A4), Color(0xFF7D5260), Color(0xFF386A20))
Box(
    Modifier.size(80.dp).clip(RoundedCornerShape(16.dp))
        .background(Brush.linearGradient(colors))
)
Box(
    Modifier.size(80.dp).clip(RoundedCornerShape(16.dp))
        .background(Brush.radialGradient(colors))
)
Box(
    Modifier.size(80.dp).clip(RoundedCornerShape(16.dp))
        .background(Brush.sweepGradient(colors))
)

components.name: Shapes & Clipping
components.summary: Rounded, circle, and cut-corner clips
components.description: Shapes define an outline used for clipping and backgrounds. RoundedCornerShape softens corners, CircleShape produces a pill or circle, and CutCornerShape bevels them. Apply with Modifier.clip then paint with a background.

examples.title: Clip outlines
examples.description: The same colored box clipped three different ways.
examples.code:
Box(
    Modifier.size(80.dp).clip(RoundedCornerShape(20.dp))
        .background(MaterialTheme.colorScheme.primary)
)
Box(
    Modifier.size(80.dp).clip(CircleShape)
        .background(MaterialTheme.colorScheme.secondary)
)
Box(
    Modifier.size(80.dp).clip(CutCornerShape(20.dp))
        .background(MaterialTheme.colorScheme.tertiary)
)

components.name: Draw Modifiers
components.summary: drawBehind, drawWithContent, and border
components.description: Draw modifiers attach custom rendering to any composable. Modifier.drawBehind paints under the content, Modifier.drawWithContent lets you sequence drawing around drawContent(), and Modifier.border strokes a shaped outline.

examples.title: Accent behind text
examples.description: drawBehind paints a highlight bar under a label.

examples.title: Overlay & border
examples.description: drawWithContent tints over the content; border strokes a shaped edge.
